candidates.name: Greg Abbott
candidates.notes: Three-term governor running for fourth term in 2026. Signed Texas Heartbeat Act (SB8) and constitutional carry. Launched Operation Lone Star border initiative. Signed school voucher legislation. Strong NRA supporter (A+ rating). Signed In God We Trust display law for schools. Paralyzed below waist since 1984 jogging accident. [2026 actions: Operation Lone Star 2.0 — shifted from pure border ops to interior immigration enforcement with ICE (~100 arrests/week on TX-Mexico border). Signed SB 8 + SB 36 expanding border-security coordination with federal gov + Homeland Security Division within TX DPS. Legislature earmarked $3.4B for border security in current 2-year budget. Confirms perfect border_immigration score.]

claims.text: Signed Texas Senate Bill 8 (the heartbeat bill, May 2021) protecting unborn children from abortion once a heartbeat is detected, and House Bill 1280 (the trigger-ban law) which took effect after Dobbs and prohibits nearly all abortions in Texas. Abbott has been a featured speaker at the Texas Rally for Life, affirming protection of human life from conception.

Launched Operation Lone Star in 2021, deploying the Texas National Guard and Texas Department of Public Safety to the southern border. Signed legislation allocating $1.54 billion for construction of border barriers. In 2023-2024 initiated busing of migrants to Democratic-led cities and deployed razor-wire barriers along the Rio Grande, becoming the national leader in state-level military border enforcement.

Signed House Bill 1927 (September 2021), making Texas a permitless-carry state allowing Texans to carry a handgun without a license or training — one of the largest Second Amendment expansions in Texas history. Abbott has also signed legislation strengthening castle-doctrine protections and consistently vetoed gun-control measures reaching his desk.

NOTE: the Permanent School Fund / BlackRock ESG divestment is Comptroller/SBOE-driven, NOT a Buckingham action — not scored. church_affiliation.notes: Backfilled from usmcmin.org/directory-politicians.html via cross-reference enrichment.

claims.text: Holds an A+ rating from SBA Pro-Life America and has a 100% pro-life voting record in the Senate. Cosponsored the Life at Conception Act and has voted against every pro-abortion bill, affirming personhood from the moment of fertilization.

Holds an A+ rating from the NRA and has been one of the Senate's most vocal opponents of gun-control proposals: led floor opposition to the Bipartisan Safer Communities Act, co-sponsored the Respect for the Second Amendment Act (S.840, 118th Congress), and publicly called colleagues who support red-flag laws and assault-weapons bans 'squishes' on the Second Amendment.

A hard-line border hawk who has introduced multiple Build the Wall bills, demanded military deployment to the southern border, and in February 2024 voted against the bipartisan border deal as far too weak — insisting on mandatory removal, physical wall completion, and an end to catch-and-release.

A consistent fiscal hawk who has voted against repeated debt-ceiling increases, opposed deficit-expanding continuing resolutions, and publicly condemned 'reckless, out-of-control spending' by Democrats. Cruz voted against the second debt ceiling increase and voted against Democrats' continuing resolutions, framing debt expansion as 'a political pawn to avoid taking accountability for more reckless, out-of-control spending' — directly aligning with the rubric's anti-deficit/balanced-budget standard.

Cruz led the group of 11 Republican senators who on January 2, 2021 issued a joint statement demanding a 10-day Emergency Electoral Commission to audit the 2020 presidential election in six disputed states, citing allegations of fraud and irregularities 'that exceed any in our lifetimes.' He objected to Arizona's electoral votes on January 6-7, 2021, forcing a roll-call vote in the Senate. He subsequently opposed the Electoral Count Reform Act of 2022, arguing it unconstitutionally reduced Congress's ability to reject fraudulent electoral-vote submissions.

Cruz voted against the Respect for Marriage Act (RFMA) when it passed the Senate in November 2022 (61-36) and again in December 2022 (61-36 final passage), which codified federal recognition of same-sex and interracial marriages. He issued a press statement explaining his opposition, and together with Senator Mike Lee co-sponsored the State Marriage Defense Act to preserve each state's right to define marriage as one man and one woman — maintaining the one-man-one-woman definition the rubric endorses.

claims.text: Carries a 100% pro-life voting record; cosponsored the Born-Alive Abortion Survivors Protection Act, which would require full medical care for infants who survive a failed abortion; and has fought to make the Hyde Amendment permanent to block taxpayer funding of abortion.

Led and shepherded the Bipartisan Safer Communities Act (signed June 2022) — the first major federal gun-control legislation in nearly 30 years — which expanded background-check waiting periods for under-21 purchasers, closed the 'boyfriend loophole,' and provided federal grants to incentivize states to enact red-flag laws. He was booed at the Republican Party of Texas convention and rebuked by the state party for his role.

A Texas border-security advocate who has consistently backed wall funding and military resources at the southern border; authored the Stop the Cartels Act to designate major drug cartels as foreign terrorist organizations and authorize military counterdrug operations.

Voted for the SAVE America Act, landmark legislation requiring proof of U.S. citizenship when registering to vote and photo identification before casting a ballot in a federal election. Cornyn also co-led a bicameral letter to the U.S. Election Assistance Commission urging that documentary proof of citizenship be added to the National Mail Voter Registration Form, arguing it would 'strengthen the integrity of our elections and ensure that every lawful vote is protected from being diluted by unlawful ballots.'

A consistent opponent of deficit spending who has authored and voted for balanced budget amendments to the U.S. Constitution multiple times, arguing Congress must be held to the same discipline as every American family. He voted against the Democrats' Inflation Reduction Act ('reckless tax-and-spend') and has written op-eds calling for immediate structural spending reform and a constitutional cap on federal outlays.

Voted against the Respect for Marriage Act (H.R. 8404, Senate Vote #362, Nov. 29, 2022) and argued that without strong amendments the bill would 'trample on religious liberties' by compelling recognition of same-sex unions. He previously co-sponsored the Federal Marriage Amendment defining marriage as one man and one woman, and told reporters at the Ketanji Brown Jackson confirmation hearings that states should retain the power to define marriage as they see fit.

claims.text: A consistent pro-life Texas legislator: co-authored HB 1500 (the Heartbeat Protection Act, banning abortion after fetal heartbeat detection) and HB 896 to prohibit abortion outright; cosponsored SB 22, signed into law in June 2019, cutting all government funding to Planned Parenthood. Running for U.S. House TX-02 in 2026 as the R nominee having defeated incumbent Dan Crenshaw, he has declared his intent to join the House Freedom Caucus.

Authored the Texas Firearm Protection Act (HB 112, 87th session), a Second Amendment sanctuary bill that would void any attempt to enforce federal gun laws in Texas not also enacted under Texas law — directly opposing federal firearm bans, registries, and restrictions. A companion bill, HB 2622, passed both chambers and was signed by Gov. Abbott on June 17, 2021.

As a member of the Texas House Appropriations Committee, Toth fought to appropriate billions of dollars for finishing the border wall and critical border security initiatives, explicitly opposing Democrats and moderate Republicans who sought to defund border construction — a record consistent with the rubric's wall-plus-military border standard.

candidates.name: Dustin Burrows
candidates.notes: Speaker of the Texas House (elected Jan 2025) representing HD83 (Lubbock); Republican rep since 2015. SPEAKER-VOTE WRINKLE: by House tradition the presiding officer usually does not cast recorded floor votes, so a low 2025 roll-call count is procedural, not ideological -- most HARD personal votes below are pre-Speaker (2015-2024). As Speaker he CHAMPIONED SB2 (2025 universal ESA/voucher) -- publicly drove passage and spoke at signing -- and his House passed SB10 (Ten Commandments), SB11 (school prayer), SJR5 (bail reform, 133-8; later Prop 3) and HB796 (Texas Sovereignty Act). Co-sponsor of SB1 (2021 Election Integrity Act); named House conferee on HB1927 (2021 permitless carry). Texas Alliance for Life credited him by name for 2025 pro-life wins. FACTIONAL / ESTABLISHMENT CATCH: won the speakership with a Republican-Democrat coalition over hard-right favorite David Cook (cast as 'a blow to the GOP's hard right'); RPT threatened censure of members who backed him; branded 'RINO' by grassroots; appointed Democrat committee chairs; 2019 'Burrowsgate' secret-recording episode cost him the House GOP Caucus chair. Texas Right to Life criticized him for the death of priority abortion-pill bill SB2880 ('the buck stops with the Speaker'). JBS Freedom Index career 61% (50% in 2023-24) corroborates the establishment framing -- offset by his authorship of the 2019 property-tax-cap SB2. Currency verified vs Ballotpedia + house.texas.gov.

candidates.name: Philip Cortez
candidates.notes: TX State Representative, HD-117 (D), multiple terms since 2012; Air Force Reserve captain. [2026-06 national refinement, state tier] Progressive on the marquee social issues but a documented CROSS-PARTY OUTLIER on several others: voted NO on SB8 (2021 heartbeat), HB1280 (trigger ban), and SB4 (Choice Tracker), is PP Texas Votes-endorsed, voted YES on HB1738 (2023 repeal of the homosexual-conduct crime), voted NO on SB2 (2025 voucher), and opposed SB1 strenuously enough to break quorum in 2021 (though he was the first Democrat to return to negotiate, and declined to break quorum at all in 2025). Conservative groups rate him low (Freedom Index 13%, Texans for Fiscal Responsibility 'F'). DEVIATIONS (broke RIGHT of the D line, per Freedom Index roll-call records): YES on HB796 (Texas Sovereignty Act / 10th-Amendment anti-commandeering), YES on HB9 (2023 border-security funds / Operation Lone Star), and YES on HB1927 (2021 permitless carry); plus YES on HB1056 sound-money and SB1070 (ERIC withdrawal). CAUTION (transparency): the HB1927 vote comes from a single secondary source (Freedom Index) and could not be re-confirmed against the TLO roll call this pass; the HB9 border vote is scored as documented support for active border presence. Texas Alliance for Life rates him 59% (consensus maternal-health bills, not a pro-life shift). Do NOT conflate the Freedom-Index '2023 SB8 - Yes' entry with the 2021 SB8 ban, which he voted NO on.

profile.background: TX House District 117 (western San Antonio/Bexar Co.), Democrat; elected 2012, lost 2014, regained 2016, re-elected since. U.S. Air Force Reserve captain; former San Antonio City Council member (2007-2011); PhD in Educational Administration (UT Austin). On Homeland Security/Public Safety/Veterans' Affairs and Intergovernmental Affairs. Considered a business-friendly moderate.

candidates.name: John Lujan
candidates.notes: TX State Representative, HD-118 (R), since the 2021 special; Baptist Sunday-school teacher and career first-responder in a swing district. [2026-06 national refinement, state tier] CURRENCY: NOT running for HD-118 re-election in 2026 — he sought U.S. House TX-35 and LOST the May 2026 Republican runoff (candidacy_status set not_running; still the sitting member through Jan 2027). Solidly socially conservative: Texas Alliance for Life 100% (2025), Texas Right to Life 86%, Texas Values Action 86%; voted YES on SB8/SB33 (life), HB229 'What is a Woman Act' and SB1257 (biological sex), SB2 (2025 voucher — the key swing-R vote, and he opposed the 2023 amendment that stripped ESA language), SB12 (Parents' Bill of Rights), SB17 (DEI ban), SB10 (Ten Commandments) and SB11 (school prayer), and HB796 (Texas Sovereignty Act); supported Operation Lone Star/border-wall funding; NRA-PVF, TSRA, and CLEAT (police union) endorsed. Election-specific cells left null (SB1070 ERIC withdrawal doesn't map cleanly to a rubric cell; not seated for SB1 2021).

candidates.name: Chad West
candidates.notes: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local tier). Verified current: re-elected May 3, 2025 with 58.78% over Katrina Whatley; term-limited after 2025-27. Pro-choice on record (endorsed 2022 GRACE Act deprioritizing abortion enforcement; absent for the vote itself). Openly gay; Stonewall Democrats endorsement; Dallas Pride grand marshal. Deviation from progressive expectation: genuinely fiscally hawkish — chaired mayor-backed push to cut spending/taxes, backed FY2025-26 tax-rate cut; publicly anti-defund since 2020 ('it's not happening now') and shepherded budgets funding 350 recruits, though he opposed 2024 Dallas HERO Prop U (4,000-officer mandate) on fiscal grounds shared by the police union. Opposed DPD joining ICE 287(g) (Oct 2025). No findable positions on schools, elections, religious liberty, state-overreach cells, or gun policy — left null.

profile.background: Attorney and small-business owner; Army veteran (Bosnia/Hungary deployments); chairs council Finance Committee. First elected 2019; re-elected May 2025 (58.8%) — final term under Dallas's 4-term limit. Openly gay.

candidates.name: Jesse Moreno
candidates.notes: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local tier). Verified current: re-elected May 3, 2025; Mayor Pro Tem since June 16, 2025 (annual reselection due ~June 2026). Absent for the 2022 GRACE Act vote but publicly endorsed it. Read the city's 2025 Pride Month proclamation. Deviation from progressive expectation: one of only 3 no votes on the FY2025-26 $5.5B budget, dissenting for deeper spending cuts and a bigger tax-rate reduction; and genuine affirmative pro-police advocacy in 2025 — unveiled the Deep Ellum crime plan (more police, mounted patrol) and pushed to RAISE DPD hiring goals — though he voted to pull $10M DPD overtime in 2021 and opposed 2024 Prop U (mixed record, credited on the recent affirmative advocacy). Opposed DPD joining ICE 287(g) (Oct 2025). Created/championed the 2023 Dallas gun-buyback program (voluntary; matches no specific self-defense cell — noted, left null). Religion/education not found.

profile.background: Co-owner of family restaurants La Popular Tamale House and Peak & Elm; third-generation District 2 resident; 7+ years on the Dallas Park Board. First elected 2021; re-elected May 2025 over Sukhbir Kaur; appointed Mayor Pro Tem June 16, 2025 (one-year term).

candidates.name: Zarin Gracey
candidates.notes: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local tier). Verified current: re-elected May 3, 2025 (~54% over Lightbourne and Sims, no runoff); term ends 2027. Openly Christian public official — X bio: 'Through service to my church and my City, I desire to boldly reflect Christ through out the City of Dallas'; former Concord Church executive pastor; a Dallas LGBT-activist publication flagged him as religiously conservative, and Stonewall Democrats' 2023 endorsement list skipped District 3 (suggestive, not scored). Fiscally frustrated with the Prop U mandate while voting for the 300-officer hiring goal (Feb 2025: 'This silly, silly loop is so extremely frustrating') — said he favors more officers (paraphrase, not credited under the high-bar rule). Voted yes on the unanimous FY2024-25 budget and (unnamed in dissent coverage) the FY2025-26 budget. Attended the Nov 6, 2025 joint-committee session that unanimously killed the $25M DPD-ICE 287(g) partnership (only West absent — joined the unanimous rejection, no individual statement). No findable positions on abortion, marriage/trans, schools, elections, state-overreach cells, or guns — left null.

profile.religion: Baptist (former Executive Pastor of Ministries, Concord Church, 2021-2023)
profile.education: B.S. Accounting, University of Arkansas at Pine Bluff; M.A. Organizational Leadership, Luther Rice Seminary; Harvard JFK School state/local govt certificate
profile.background: ~16 years in Dallas City Hall finance roles (Debt Manager, Cash & Investment Manager, Director of the Office of Business Diversity 2017-2021); municipal finance at First Southwest Co.; Executive Pastor of Ministries at Concord Church 2021-2023. First elected June 2023 (runoff over Joe Tave); re-elected May 3, 2025 (~54%, no runoff). Married to Erin; two adopted sons. D3 = southwest Dallas/west Oak Cliff.

candidates.name: Maxie Johnson
candidates.notes: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local tier). Verified current: won May 3, 2025 outright (~75%) over Alexander and Hardaman, unseating Carolyn King Arnold; seated June 16, 2025; chaired the joint Public Safety/Government Efficiency committee through the 287(g) fight. Corrected stale background (he is D4 south Oak Cliff, not Pleasant Grove; no runoff). Led the Nov 6, 2025 unanimous committee kill of the $25M DPD-ICE 287(g) partnership with hard quotes: 'We're not gonna support racism, we're not gonna take money cause all money ain't good money' and 'it's hard for me to support any organization that rips apart homes based on skin color.' As DISD trustee, used his work email (Aug 2024) to declare 'I have always believed, and continue to advocate, that public funds belong in public schools,' opposing the Governor's school-choice push — documented contrary on school choice. Delivers openly Christian invocation prayer at council (Jan 14, 2026 session video). DISD board rejected SB 763 school chaplains 7-0 with one unnamed absence — his individual vote unconfirmed, not scored. Dallas Morning News + Dallas Neighbors for Housing endorsements 2025; Stonewall Democrats screening outcome unconfirmed — not scored. No findable positions on abortion, marriage/trans, elections, state-overreach, or guns — left null. FY2025-26 budget: not individually named in coverage — not scored.

candidates.notes: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local tier). Verified current: re-elected May 3, 2025 with 84%; term-limited after this term. Present for the 13-1 GRACE Act vote (Aug 2022, deprioritizing abortion investigations; sole no was McGough — Resendez among the yes votes). Stonewall Democrats of Dallas endorsement. candidates.name: Laura Cadena
candidates.notes: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local tier). Campaign platform: 'Hire more police officers & strengthen community trust' plus retention incentives — affirmative pro-police advocacy, reinforced in office (Dec 2025 praise for the neighborhood police officer program). candidates.name: Kathy Stewart
candidates.notes: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local tier). Verified current: re-elected May 3, 2025 (~92.5%), sworn in June 2025. PARTY FLAG: the 'R' label is unsubstantiated — Dallas council is nonpartisan, she told a candidate questionnaire 'it's essential to remain nonpartisan,' and her endorsements were business/real-estate (TREC PAC, MetroTex Realtors), not partisan; label retained but unverified. Genuine affirmative pro-police record: proposed the 325-officer/yr DPD hiring-goal compromise (Feb 2025), praised the police/fire pension funding deal for ending recruitment 'uncertainty,' championed the neighborhood police officer program (Dec 2025). Opposed Dallas HERO Props S/T/U on budget grounds shared with the police union ('devastating to the city budget... and to police officers who could lose their immunity'). 287(g): issued a neutral statement supporting officers and the chief; joined the unanimous committee postponement that shelved the ICE partnership — left null (declining a voluntary partnership is not a sanctuary policy; individual roll call unverified). Co-signed concerns the 2024 marijuana-decriminalization charter amendment 'appears to be straight-up illegal' (legality concern, not scored). Voted for the FY2025-26 budget with the 10th straight tax-rate cut (routine governance, not scored). No findable positions on abortion, marriage/gender, school choice, religious liberty, elections, state overreach, or guns — left null; sparse denominator noted. Religion not documented.

HARD contrary evidence: authored the GRACE Act (July 2022, passed unanimously) making abortion-related enforcement APD's lowest priority and called the state ban 'draconian'; introduced the May 2024 resolution (passed 10-1) deprioritizing SB 14 enforcement (gender-transition treatments for minors) — first Texas city — saying 'Trans people deserve the right to self determination'; presented the 'Y'all Means All Day' LGBT proclamation; part of the Aug 2025 subquorum proposing a 6.75-cent property-tax increase and joined the 5-cent tax-rate-election motion (voters rejected it Nov 2025; still favors taxing to the 3.5% state cap); champion of marijuana-enforcement deprioritization and praised Travis County personal-bond magistration ('probably the best magistration system in the state'); led the Jan 2026 APD policy restricting ICE cooperation ('communication with ICE should be an exceptional circumstance') — now under a Paxton investigation. Credit where earned (public_justice q0 TRUE): voted YES on the 5-year $218M police contract (Oct 2024, 10-1), publicly calling it a 'major, major win' that 'accomplishes everything the APOA requires,' and publicly praised police/EMS/fire response to the 2026 6th Street mass shooting — affirmative public backing, no defund support documented (joined council after the 2020 budget cut); also advocates police-oversight enforcement and delayed transfer of 37 civilian positions to APD (2023). refuse_state_overreach left null: his repeated resistance to TX state law (abortion, SB 14, marijuana, SB4/ICE) runs in the progressive direction against the rubric's intent. No public religious profession found.

profile.education: BA (history), MPA, JD — University of Texas at Austin
profile.background: Immigration/criminal-defense attorney (represented hundreds in deportation/asylum proceedings); former board chair, Workers Defense Project; won the Jan 2022 D4 special election, re-elected Nov 2024 (58.5%); serving as Mayor Pro Tem in 2026; term ends Jan 2029.

candidates.name: Ryan Alter
candidates.notes: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local autopilot, Austin batch-3). Sitting D5 member, web-verified June 2026 (quoted in role May 2026); fundraising for the Nov 2026 ballot. In the 7-vote majority on the Aug 2024 FY2025 abortion-travel-fund amendment (the four no votes were named and he was not among them). Co-sponsored the May 2024 SB 14 trans-protections resolution (first Texas city to deprioritize enforcement). Publicly campaigned for the Prop Q 5-cent tax-rate increase, calling its 63% defeat the loss of a 'lifeline.' On HB 2127 preemption: 'This bill really is a true threat to what cities can do.' Defended Austin's policy limiting ICE cooperation on administrative warrants and signed the 2026 council letter against 'unconstitutional and violent federal operations.' Public-justice cells held to the high bar: his police-contract posture was oversight-conditional (would vote down any contract not implementing the Police Oversight Act) plus a quarterly police-spend transparency resolution — neither affirmative pro-police backing nor defund advocacy → null. Voted yes on HOME zoning density reform (Dec 2023, 9-2). Gun cells null — the red-flag advocacy in Austin coverage belongs to Alison Alter, not him.

profile.education: BS Engineering, University of Texas at Austin; JD Harvard Law School
profile.background: Austin City Council D5 (south-central Austin/Zilker) since Jan 2023. Attorney; former Texas Senate staffer (Watson, Garcia, Hinojosa). Married, three children. Running for re-election Nov 2026. Not related to former D10 member Alison Alter.

candidates.notes: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local autopilot, Austin batch). Sitting D6 member, web-verified June 2026. Short council record (took office Jan 2025) but a consistent progressive profile scored from her own endorsements page, Ballotpedia survey, and council votes: Planned Parenthood Texas Votes + Stonewall Democrats endorsements; on-record trans-defense pledge ('I will make sure trans people in Austin are defended,' Nov 2024); co-founded the PAC that swept the anti-CRT/book-removal Round Rock ISD slate, in her own words defeating 'extremist Moms For Liberty, Christian Nationalists'; organized against vouchers and for increased public-school funding; co-proposed a 5.75-cent tax-rate hike and voted for the $6.3B FY26 budget + Prop Q (rejected by voters); signed the Jan 2026 joint statement calling ICE 'a malicious agency... no place in Austin'; lists Everytown/Moms Demand 'Gun Sense Candidate' designation. Public-justice q0 held to the high bar → null: ran on 911 response and 'public safety with accountability,' voted the largest-ever APD allotment, no defund rhetoric — but no affirmative pro-police advocacy found. Serves on the council Public Safety Committee.

candidates.name: Mike Siegel
candidates.notes: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local autopilot, Austin batch). Sitting D7 member, web-verified June 2026 (sponsored the TRUST Act surveillance-oversight ordinance enacted Apr 23, 2026). Deep documented progressive record: personally argued before the Texas Supreme Court defending the San Antonio 'Justice Charter' (abortion + marijuana decriminalization, cite-and-release, no-knock/chokehold bans); authored the Dec 2025 anti-SB 8 'bathroom bill' response resolution (trans-welcoming restrooms; called the law 'motivated by discriminatory animus,' threatened legal recourse); co-proposed the 5-cent tax-rate hike that became the failed Prop Q; co-authored the Austin Police Oversight Act and sued the city for Equity Action to enforce it ('invest in a broad array of programs to promote public safety, not just officers with guns'); led the SB 4 sanctuary-city litigation against Abbott; Giffords-endorsed in 2020 (background checks, assault-weapons ban, red-flag laws); 2026 response to Paxton's ICE-policy probe: 'These attacks by the disgraced Texas attorney general only strengthen our resolve.' Held to discipline: school-voucher opposition only softly evidenced (critical X post on Yass donation) → null; 2018 Waller County early-voting fight is access advocacy, not a voter-ID position → null; no personal statement found on Austin's 2020 police budget cut (pre-tenure) — q0 FALSE rests on his own documented oversight/reform record.

profile.education: BA Brandeis University; JD Cornell Law School
profile.background: Austin City Council D7 (north-central Austin) since Jan 2025; won the Dec 14, 2024 runoff over Gary Bledsoe by 206 votes; term to Jan 2029. Former public-school teacher, civil-rights lawyer, and Assistant Austin City Attorney (2015–2019) — lead city lawyer suing Gov. Abbott over SB 4, the anti-sanctuary-city law. Twice Democratic nominee vs. Rep. Michael McCaul (TX-10, 2018/2020; Sanders- and Warren-endorsed in 2020). Co-founded Ground Game Texas (marijuana-decrim and abortion-decrim local ballot measures); co-authored the Austin Police Oversight Act. Austin DSA lists him among socialists it helped elect — the council's furthest-left member.

candidates.name: Paige Ellis
candidates.notes: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local autopilot, Austin batch-3). Sitting D8 member, web-verified June 2026; on the Nov 2026 ballot. Co-sponsored the GRACE Act (July 2022): 'This council is committed to and will continue to support our constituents who overwhelmingly agree that people should have access to abortion.' (One opposition-side report listed her as a NO on the Aug 2024 abortion-travel amendment while another called her a co-sponsor — contested, excluded from scoring.) Co-sponsored the intersex-surgery condemnation resolution; on council for the May 2024 SB 14 trans-protections resolution (passed 10-1, she was not the dissenter). Voted for the unanimous Aug 2020 ~$150M APD cut/divert, then the state-compelled 2021 restoration. Co-proposed the 5-cent Prop Q tax-rate increase (rejected by voters Nov 2025) and voted for the $6.3B budget triggering it. Jan 2026 joint statement after an APD-assisted ICE deportation: 'These ICE deportations are wrong.' Co-sponsored 2022 resolutions on gun violence including restricting AR-15 sales to under-21s and a free gun-lock program.
